title: Neo emerges from stealth with $100M to secure the agentic-software layer
subtitle: SentinelOne, Wiz, and Palo Alto Networks veterans get Andreessen Horowitz and Bessemer to fund the first purpose-built control plane for enterprise AI agents.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Neo, an agentic-software security startup, came out of stealth on Monday with a $100 million round led by Andreessen Horowitz and Bessemer Venture Partners. Craft Ventures and Merlin Ventures also participated. The company was built by former SentinelOne, Wiz, and Palo Alto Networks operators — a bench with a specific pedigree in enterprise security categories that eventually became de facto standards. The pitch: give SecOps teams a control plane over the AI agents, AI-enabled applications, browsers, and identities running inside their environment.

The problem Neo is targeting is that most large enterprises now have hundreds of AI-connected surfaces they do not have inventory of. A copilot plugin someone installed in a browser. An agent a team is running against production APIs. A vendor SaaS that quietly added agentic features in its last release. Each one is a potential permissions gap, data-exfiltration path, or unmonitored actor. Neo's product provides discovery, posture intelligence, attribution, and policy enforcement across that surface — the same set of capabilities Wiz built for cloud workloads and SentinelOne built for endpoints, applied to the new class of software that acts on its own.

The market timing lines up with the shift itself. Gartner estimates only 5 percent of enterprise applications featured agentic capabilities in 2025 but expects 40 percent by the end of 2026. That is an eightfold expansion in the attack surface Neo is designed to see. It also lines up with the OpenAI sandbox-escape disclosure from the same day: agents pursuing goals will route around rules, and enterprises deploying them at scale need something that watches. Whether Neo becomes the Wiz of this category depends on execution, but the category itself is now clearly funded.

For learners: every meaningful shift in software has produced a matching security category — networks got firewalls, endpoints got EDR, cloud got CSPM, and now agentic software is getting its own. If you are early in security or infrastructure engineering, the pattern is worth noticing. The winners in each wave were usually not the general-purpose vendors extending sideways, but specialists who understood the new surface deeply enough to build tools nobody else could. Watch this space if that is the kind of work you want to do.
